threats.title: Protocol Bricked at Generation 2 due to initializeAlphaIndices Access Control
threats.hypothesis_description: TraitForgeNft._incrementGeneration calls entropyGenerator.initializeAlphaIndices(), which is protected by the onlyOwner modifier. Since TraitForgeNft is set as the allowedCaller but is not the owner of EntropyGenerator, this call will always revert, permanently preventing the protocol from advancing past Generation 1.
threats.investigation_rationale: The deployment script sets the deployer as the owner of EntropyGenerator. When 10,000 tokens are minted, the generation increment will fail, halting all future mints.
deployment_assumptions: TraitForgeNft is not transferred ownership of EntropyGenerator after deployment
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts lines 22-25 show EntropyGenerator deployed with nft address as constructor arg (allowedCaller), but ownership is never transferred to TraitForgeNft.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onlyOwner modifier on function called by another contract

preconditions: TraitForgeNft is not the owner of EntropyGenerator

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: External call to onlyOwner function from non-owner contract
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Use of onlyAllowedCaller instead of onlyOwner

analysis_questions: Is ownership of EntropyGenerator ever transferred to TraitForgeNft?
Can the owner manually call initializeAlphaIndices to bypass this?
validation_requirements: Test generation increment in a local fork

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Once 10,000 tokens are minted, any user calling mintToken will trigger _incrementGeneration, which reverts. The protocol is permanently locked.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Loss of all future protocol revenue from minting
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: All users attempting to mint after Gen 1
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires contract upgrade or owner manually transferring ownership of EntropyGenerator to TraitForgeNft

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing integration test for generation increment

threats.title: mintWithBudget Permanently Disabled After Generation 1
threats.hypothesis_description: The while loop in mintWithBudget uses the global _tokenIds counter instead of the generation-specific counter. Once 10,000 tokens are minted globally, the loop condition will always evaluate to false.
threats.investigation_rationale: _tokenIds strictly increases across all generations, while maxTokensPerGen is a static limit (10,000) per generation. Comparing a global counter to a per-generation limit breaks the logic.
preconditions: More than 10,000 tokens have been minted globally (_tokenIds >= 10000)

analysis_questions: Does mintWithBudget function at all after Generation 1?
validation_requirements: Simulate minting past 10,000 tokens and call mintWithBudget

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users attempting to use mintWithBudget in Generation 2+ will simply have their ETH refunded without receiving any tokens, forcing them to use the single-mint function.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Reduced minting velocity and higher gas costs for users
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Cannot be recovered without contract upgrade

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for mintWithBudget in Generation 2

threats.title: Entropy Sequence Repeats Exactly Across Generations
threats.hypothesis_description: EntropyGenerator wraps its slot index back to 0 after 10,010 values are consumed, without regenerating the entropy slots. This causes Generation 2+ to receive the exact same sequence of traits as Generation 1.
threats.investigation_rationale: Since the entropy array is static after initialization and getPublicEntropy is public, attackers can perfectly predict the traits of all future tokens once the index wraps.
preconditions: More than 10,010 tokens minted across the protocol

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to re-roll entropy slots

analysis_questions: Are entropy slots ever updated after the initial batch writes?
validation_requirements: Trace entropy values for tokens 1 and 10011 to confirm they are identical

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker maps the exact sequence of Generation 1 traits. In Generation 2, they time their mints to snipe the highest nuke factor and forge potential tokens.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Severe extraction of NukeFund by snipers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Honest minters who receive suboptimal traits

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for entropy uniqueness across generations

threats.title: Entities Can Exceed Their Forge Potential Due to Off-By-One Error
threats.hypothesis_description: EntityForging.listForForging uses a '<=' operator when checking forgingCounts against forgePotential. Because forgeWithListed increments the count after this check, entities can forge one time more than their maximum potential.
threats.investigation_rationale: An entity with forgePotential of 1 can list when count is 1 (since 1 <= 1), and then forge, bringing its count to 2. This inflates the supply of forged entities.

analysis_questions: Can a token with forgePotential 1 forge 2 times?
validation_requirements: Test forging loop until revert and count successful forges

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user repeatedly lists and forges their entity, extracting one extra forge fee and creating one extra entity beyond the intended limit.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Inflation of entity supply and unauthorized fee extraction

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing boundary tests for forge limits

threats.title: NukeFund Age Calculation Always Returns Zero
threats.hypothesis_description: The ageMultiplier in NukeFund is 0 by default and is never initialized in the deployment script. This causes the entire age calculation to multiply by 0, completely disabling the hold-to-earn mechanic.
threats.investigation_rationale: Users can nuke high-entropy tokens immediately after the minimum hold period for maximum value, as holding them longer provides zero additional benefit.
threats.config_evidence: deployTasks.ts does not contain any call to setAgeMultplier on the NukeFund contract.

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Nullifies the age component of the nuke factor

analysis_questions: Is ageMultiplier set anywhere in the deployment pipeline?
validation_requirements: Check calculateAge output on a deployed instance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Users mint high-entropy tokens and nuke them as soon as minimumDaysHeld passes, extracting funds without participating in the long-term holding economy.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Long-term holders who lose out to short-term extractors
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Owner can call setAgeMultplier to fix future calculations

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for age calculation with default deployment state

threats.title: Excess ETH Sent to forgeWithListed is Permanently Trapped
threats.hypothesis_description: forgeWithListed requires msg.value >= forgingFee but does not refund the difference. Any excess ETH sent by the user is permanently locked in the contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: There is no sweep or withdraw function in EntityForging, so any overpayment is unrecoverable.

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Excess ETH is not returned to msg.sender

analysis_questions: Is there any way to recover trapped ETH from EntityForging?
validation_requirements: Send excess ETH to forgeWithListed and check contract balance

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user accidentally sends 1 ETH instead of 0.1 ETH for a forging fee. The 0.9 ETH is permanently trapped in the contract.

threats.title: Pausable Mechanism is Inaccessible
threats.hypothesis_description: All core contracts inherit OpenZeppelin's Pausable and use whenNotPaused modifiers, but none implement an external function to call _pause(). The protocol cannot be paused during an emergency.
threats.investigation_rationale: The emergency stop mechanism is a critical security control. Its absence means attackers can continue exploiting a vulnerability even after it is discovered.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Inherits Pausable but lacks pause() wrapper

preconditions: An emergency requires pausing the protocol

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: whenNotPaused modifiers are effectively dead code

analysis_questions: Is there any hidden mechanism to trigger a pause?
validation_requirements: Verify absence of pause wrappers in all contracts

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A vulnerability is actively exploited. The admin attempts to pause the protocol but realizes no such function exists, leading to total fund loss.
threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for admin pause functionality

threats.title: Stale listedTokenIds Mapping Causes Inconsistent Listing State
threats.hypothesis_description: When a forging listing is cancelled, the listing struct is deleted but listedTokenIds[tokenId] is not cleared. This leaves a stale pointer that can cause getListedTokenIds to return a non-zero index for a cancelled listing.
threats.investigation_rationale: Stale state can lead to unexpected behavior in hooks like _beforeTokenTransfer, which relies on getListedTokenIds to determine if a token is listed.
preconditions: A token is listed for forging and then cancelled

analysis_questions: Can the stale index be exploited to bypass listing checks?
Does _beforeTokenTransfer behave incorrectly due to the stale index?
validation_requirements: Test relisting and transferring cancelled tokens

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A user cancels a listing, but the stale index causes _beforeTokenTransfer to attempt cancellation again during a transfer, potentially reverting if the logic doesn't handle the empty struct gracefully.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: None directly, but could cause DoS on token transfers
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Relisting the token overwrites the stale index

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for transferring tokens after listing cancellation

threats.title: Setting taxCut to Zero Bricks Protocol Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: If the owner sets taxCut to 0, any function that distributes fees (minting, forging, trading) will revert due to division by zero in NukeFund.receive or EntityForging.forgeWithListed.
threats.investigation_rationale: While this requires admin action, the impact is a complete halt of all economic activity in the protocol.

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: No validation preventing taxCut from being 0

analysis_questions: Are there any safeguards preventing taxCut from being set to 0?
validation_requirements: Call setTaxCut(0) and attempt to mint or forge

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The admin accidentally sets taxCut to 0. All subsequent mints, trades, and forges revert, completely bricking the protocol until the admin corrects the value.
prerequisites: Admin error or compromised admin key

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary halt of all protocol revenue and operations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Admin can set taxCut back to > 0
